title: On the Distribution of Modular Points on Deformation Rings
speaker: John Bergdall
abstract: The goal of this talk is to discuss a new local-global phenomenon in the Langlands program on automorphic forms and Galois representations. Spaces of modular forms are parametrized by weights, and those same weights parametrize local deformation rings defined by conditions in p-adic Hodge theory. In the mid-2000's, M. Kisin's work on modularity showed that modular points can be found on each and every component of one of these local deformation rings. The purpose of this talk is to describe an extension of Kisin's result, by giving a specific method for counting how many points a given component has in terms of that component's special fiber geometry. This is joint work with Chengyang Bao (Imperial College, London) and Brandon Levin (Rice University, Houston).

proof_stages.label: Statistical motivation from modular-form eigenvalues and p-adic slopes
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames p-adic slopes of modular forms as an analogue of Archimedean Sato-Tate and vertical equidistribution, introduces Gouvêa’s normalized uniform-distribution question, and explains the recent LTXZ progress and the remaining need for a conceptual explanation.

proof_stages.label: Crystalline deformation spaces as geometric receptacles for slope data
proof_stages.summary: The Ap-parameter for two-dimensional crystalline local Galois representations turns fixed-residual-class slope data into regions of a p-adic disk, realized as generic fibers of crystalline deformation rings whose special fibers carry multiplicity data.

proof_stages.label: Main componentwise modular-point count
proof_stages.summary: The central theorem asserts that, under modularity and multiplicity-one hypotheses, the number of modular points on each integral component of the generic fiber equals the Hilbert-Samuel multiplicity of the corresponding special fiber component, giving a local-to-global measure for distribution.

proof_stages.label: Total count via Serre weights and Breuil-Mézard cycles
proof_stages.summary: The proof first uses irreducibility, smoothness away from p, and a low-weight multiplicity-one assumption so that mod p cohomological counts of modular lifts match the total Breuil-Mézard special-fiber multiplicity.

proof_stages.label: Componentwise inequality from Taylor-Wiles-Kisin patching
proof_stages.summary: Patching spreads the finite modular-point count into an M-infinity module over the local deformation ring, and rank comparisons from the original modular fiber to generic and special fibers give componentwise inequalities which, together with the total count, force equality on every component.

proof_stages.label: Local application to lower bounds for components
proof_stages.summary: Using local analogues of patched modules, LTXZ’s constancy of valuation on components, and ghost-conjecture control of Hecke Newton polygons, the theorem yields explicit linear lower bounds for the number of components and formally smooth components of certain deformation rings.

proof_stages.label: Extensions and removal of simplifying hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how dropping multiplicity one replaces the clean equality by weighted counts and how the same patching-counting mechanism extends, given suitable local-global input, to Hilbert modular, semistable, and some GL_n settings.
